edge: Cross-modal alignment is essential for VLP models to learn correct corresponding information across different modalities.
Numerous masked modeling tasks have been proposed for VLP to promote cross-modal interactions.
The core idea of previous masked modeling tasks is to focus on reconstructing masked tokens for learning local-to-local alignment.
In this paper, a novel SCL task is proposed as complementary to existing masked modeling tasks to facilitate global-to-local alignment.
SCL task complements the missing semantics of masked data by capturing corresponding information from the other modality.
SCL task promotes learning more representative global features, impacting the performance of downstream tasks.
Flexible vision encoder enables the model to perform image-text and video-text multimodal tasks simultaneously.
Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on vision-language benchmarks.
proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on various vision-language benchmarks
proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on visual question answering tasks
proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on image-text retrieval tasks
proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on video-text retrieval tasks
multimodal representation learning bridges the heterogeneity gap between different modalities
vision-language pre-training models show impressive semantic alignment ability on visual question answering tasks
vision-language pre-training models show impressive semantic alignment ability on image-text retrieval tasks
self-supervised vision-language pre-training models use various masked modeling tasks to model interactions between vision and language features
MLM and MVM are both self-reconstructing masked tokens by leveraging informative visible tokens to realize local-to-local alignment.
MLM, adopted by BERT, predicts the original vocabulary IDs of masked words.
There is a trend to extend MLM to visual pre-training tasks by masking visual patches and predicting original pixels, corresponding discrete tokens, or features.
MVM tasks focus on reconstructing local masked tokens but neglect global semantic information.
Reconstructing local masked tokens may lead to inadequate learning of global representations for cross-modal information.
Global semantic features have a significant impact on pre-training model performance for downstream tasks.
The performance of the pre-training model is usually evaluated based on its ability to deal with downstream tasks.
It is crucial to ensure global semantic features to learn accurate global-to-local alignment.
Paired vision and text data represent two views of the same semantic information.
The missing semantics of masked data can be completed by capturing information from the other modality.
SCL is composed of two dual parts: masked vision semantic completion (MVSC) and masked language semantic completion (MLSC).
MVSC (MLSC) exploits information of complete text (vision) data to recover global semantic representations of masked vision (text) data.
The attention maps with SCL pre-training are more discriminative and reasonable compared to the model pre-trained without SCL.

The [CLS] feature of each frame is treated as a bridge connecting spatial modeling within the frame and temporal modeling among frames, showing a functional relationship between the vision encoder and the [CLS] feature.

The model is trained successively with both image-text and video-text datasets to transfer visual knowledge from images to videos, indicating a learning transfer relationship between the two modalities.
SCL is proposed as a pre-training task to enhance the global-to-local alignment of global representations, suggesting a relationship of improvement or enhancement of global features.
The vision encoder is described as able to transfer multimodal pre-training knowledge between images and videos readily, highlighting a relationship of knowledge transfer or sharing.

SCL is mentioned to achieve state-of-the-art performance based on a similar pre-training data scale with the help of the vision encoder, indicating a positive relationship between SCL and the vision encoder.

The dual-tower architecture based methods employ two individual encoders to separately extract the features for the visual data and textual data, while the cross-fusion architecture based methods employ a cross-modal fusion encoder to enhance the interactions between vision and text features.
CLIP exploits contrastive learning with a huge quantity of noisy image-text pairs directly collected from the Internet, achieving remarkable results on vision-language tasks.
FROZEN proposes a curriculum learning schedule to train the vision-language model on both image-text and video-text datasets by treating an image as a single-frame video.
ALBEF aligns the image and text features with contrastive learning and feeds them into a cross-modal attention-based encoder to obtain the fused features.
The cross-modal attention-based encoder receives input from contrastive learning, enhancing the fused features through attention mechanisms.
Clover focuses on improving cross-modal feature alignment through a tri-modal alignment pre-training task.
The model conducts multimodal feature fusion, enhancing performance on various downstream tasks.
The model follows the Semantic Completion Learning (SCL) approach to process image-text pairs for semantic completion.
The image-text pair is utilized for masked vision/language semantic completion tasks in a forward propagated manner.
Masked modeling tasks focus on self-reconstructing masked data to improve model performance.
BERT utilizes Masked Language modeling (MLM) to achieve state-of-the-art results on natural language processing tasks.
VLMAE introduces Masked vision modeling (MVM) with a focus on Regional Masked Image Modeling (RMIM) for feature fusion.
The input image is used to reconstruct the original pixels depending on visible patches and corresponding text.
The masked visual-token modeling task maps original video frame patches into discrete visual tokens and recovers corresponding visual tokens of masked patches for joint encoder training.
SCL task focuses on recovering global semantic information of masked data after cross-modal interactions, unlike tasks that only reconstruct local masked tokens.

In the pre-training tasks, Contrastive Learning (CL) involves projecting input images and texts into vision and language embedding spaces to adjust the positions of semantic features and enforce feature closeness for image-text pairs.
Pre-training tasks include Vision-Text Matching (VTM), aiming to determine the correspondence of an image-text pair through binary classification on concatenated vision features.
The key relation between 'correspondence' and 'image-text pair' is that the correspondence refers to the relationship or connection between an image and a text pair.
The 'binary classification' is performed on the 'concatenation' of vision and text global representations for alignment of different modalities.
MLM was used as a pretext task in 'natural language processing' and then introduced to multi-modal pre-training.
The 'reconstruction task' is important in how the 'model learns' to associate linguistic words and visual entities in local-to-local semantic alignment.
SCL is significant for the model to 'learn multi-modal information fusion' and 'extract knowledge' from the other modality.
For each data pair, masking the image and text separately allows the masked data to 'learn semantic information' from the other complete modality.

Mask ratios of SCL are observed in Table 7, indicating the importance of mask ratio in the context of SCL.
The mask ratios of image and text affect downstream tasks, especially on zero-shot retrieval. Model performance is influenced by the image mask ratio and text mask ratio.
When the text mask ratio is set to 0.4, the results of the image mask ratio of 0.8 are the highest. The mask ratio plays a crucial role in semantic completion and learning across modalities.
Choosing 0.4 as the text mask ratio and 0.8 as the image mask ratio has been found effective in the study. Different combinations of mask ratios impact cross-modal interactions and performance.
